definition: Central bank balance sheets never fully normalize after QE episodes, creating a permanent upward ratchet. Institutional endogeneity — floor system reserve requirements, LCR demand, NBFI backstop expectations — creates structural dependencies preventing return to prior levels. The moderate-form claim (permanently larger than pre-episode) is high confidence; the strong-form (ratchets upward indefinitely) is weaker.
evidence: 0/6 QE episodes followed by full normalization
Bayesian posterior (Jeffrey's prior, Beta(0.5,0.5)): 95% credible interval for full normalization probability is [0%, 21%]
Floor system reserve requirements create structural dependency on large balance sheets
LCR regulatory demand locks in elevated reserve levels
NBFI backstop expectations embed central bank accommodation into market structure
open_questions: Does the strong-form claim (ratchets upward indefinitely) hold, or only the moderate-form (permanently larger than pre-episode levels)?
What is the minimum steady-state balance sheet size implied by the ratchet for each G3 central bank?
Does the ratchet apply equally to all balance sheet components or primarily to reserves?
